questions.question: If you deploy a standard, default Node.js application to a highly expensive server with 16 CPU cores, how many cores will the application actually utilize to execute JavaScript?
options: All 16 cores due to the Event Loop
It depends on the number of users connected
Exactly 1 core
4 cores, as Node has a default thread pool of 4
questions.explanation: Node.js JavaScript execution is strictly single-threaded. Regardless of having 16 cores, a standard Node app will max out one core at 100% while the other 15 sit idle, unless intentional clustering is used.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Node.js native 'cluster' module?
options: To group similar database records together for faster querying
To fork multiple child processes (workers) that share the same server port, allowing Node to utilize all available CPU cores
To combine CSS and JavaScript files into a single bundle
To connect Node.js to a Redis caching cluster
questions.answer: To fork multiple child processes (workers) that share the same server port, allowing Node to utilize all available CPU cores
questions.explanation: The cluster module creates a primary master process that spins up child workers (one per CPU core). The master automatically load balances incoming HTTP connections across these workers in a round-robin fashion.

questions.question: Why is PM2 widely considered the community standard for deploying Node.js apps in production?
options: It acts as a process manager that automatically handles clustering, logging, and restarting the application instantly if it crashes due to a fatal error
It replaces the Express.js routing engine with a faster Rust alternative
It prevents SQL injection natively
It serves React frontend static files
questions.answer: It acts as a process manager that automatically handles clustering, logging, and restarting the application instantly if it crashes due to a fatal error
questions.explanation: PM2 daemonizes your application. If a critical bug crashes your server, PM2 restarts it in milliseconds. It also allows you to scale to all CPU cores silently with a simple '-i max' flag.

questions.question: What architectural role does NGINX usually play when placed in front of a Node.js server?
options: A relational database to replace MySQL
A Reverse Proxy that handles HTTPS decryption, rate limiting, and serves static files (like images), proxying dynamic API requests back to Node
A JavaScript execution engine that replaces V8
An automated deployment pipeline
questions.answer: A Reverse Proxy that handles HTTPS decryption, rate limiting, and serves static files (like images), proxying dynamic API requests back to Node
questions.explanation: Node.js is poor at managing SSL certificates and serving static CSS/Image files efficiently. Nginx handles these heavy network-level tasks at blistering speeds, only bothering the Node server for actual API backend logic.

questions.question: When scaling a Node application across multiple workers or physical servers, why must the application remain strictly 'stateless' (e.g., storing sessions in Redis instead of RAM)?
options: Because stateful applications are forbidden by HTTP protocol rules
Because RAM is too expensive on cloud providers
Because if User A's session is stored in the RAM of Worker 1, and their next request is load-balanced to Worker 2, Worker 2 will not recognize them and force them to log in again
Because memory storage limits string sizes to 256 characters
questions.answer: Because if User A's session is stored in the RAM of Worker 1, and their next request is load-balanced to Worker 2, Worker 2 will not recognize them and force them to log in again
questions.explanation: Every clustered worker is a completely separate V8 instance with its own isolated memory bucket. True scalability requires all shared state (sessions, caches, websockets) to be pushed out to a central external database like Redis.
